evals.prompt: Analyze this paper for me: [Attention Is All You Need, Vaswani et al. 2017 — paste full text or provide URL]
evals.expected_output: Structured 6-point analysis covering Task (sequence-to-sequence transduction), Challenge (RNN sequential dependency, vanishing gradient), Insights (self-attention as global dependency mechanism), Novelty (multi-head attention, positional encoding), Potential Flaws (quadratic complexity, fixed-length context), Motivation (can we remove recurrence entirely and rely solely on attention?). Code section should point to official transformer implementations.
expectations: Output contains all 6 sections: Task, Challenge, Insight & Novelty, Potential Flaw, Motivation, and Code
Task section formally defines input/output and constraints in plain text without LaTeX
Novelty section uses the exact three-part arrow format for each novelty point
Potential Flaw section covers at least 2 directions with root cause analysis
Motivation section contains at least one question in the 'prior methods do X, could we try Z' format
No hallucinated claims — all paper-based assertions are attributed to the paper
Code section either finds the official repo or states it was not found

evals.prompt: 帮我深度分析这篇论文：LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
evals.expected_output: Full 6-point analysis in Chinese (since the user wrote in Chinese). Task: adapting large pretrained models to downstream tasks with minimal trainable parameters. Challenge: full fine-tuning cost, catastrophic forgetting, storage per task. Insight: weight updates during adaptation have low intrinsic rank. Novelty: injecting trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into each layer. Potential flaws: rank selection sensitivity, not all layers benefit equally. Motivation: if updates are low-rank, why train all parameters?
expectations: Task section formalizes the problem: input (pretrained model + task data), output (adapted model), constraint (minimal new parameters)
Novelty uses the three-part arrow format adapted to Chinese
Insight 3.2 identifies the low-rank hypothesis as the core insight
Potential Flaw section explores rank sensitivity and layer heterogeneity as distinct directions
Code section references the official LoRA GitHub repository

evals.prompt: Analyze this paper and also I found the code here: https://github.com/some/repo — [paper about a novel graph neural network architecture]
evals.expected_output: 6-point analysis of the GNN paper, plus a code section that uses the user-provided repository link, identifies the core GNN layer implementation, and explains it concisely.
expectations: Code section uses the user-provided link, not a searched one
Code explanation points to specific file/class/function names from the actual repo
No fabricated code details — only what is verifiable in the repo
Domain expertise persona reflects GNN/graph learning field knowledge
